title: John 14
subtitle: I Am the Way, the Truth, and the Life

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — The Last Supper

sections.header: Verses 1–14 — I Am the Way, the Truth, and the Life; The Father’s House

heb.word: egō eimi hē hodos kai hē alētheia kai hē zōē
heb.transliteration: e-GO ei-MI hē ho-DOS kai hē a-LE-thei-a kai hē ZO-ē
heb.gloss: I am the way and the truth and the life
heb.paragraph: The sixth I am saying with predicate (v.6) — the most comprehensive and the most exclusive. Three nouns, each definite: the way (not a way), the truth (not a truth), the life (not a form of life). The three are sequential: the way is the path to the Father; the truth is the reality about the Father; the life is the existence derived from the Father. Jesus is all three simultaneously. The exclusive claim ("no one comes to the Father except through me") is the logical consequence of the triple definite article.

Monē (v.2) comes from the verb menō (to abide/remain) — the "many rooms" are literally "many remaining-places" or "many abiding-places." The word connects to John's dominant abiding theme (ch.15): the disciples are to abide in Christ; the Father's house has many abiding-places for those who do. The image is not of a crowded hotel but of a family home with abundant room for all who belong.

refs.note: "I will dwell in the house of the Lord forever" — the Psalm that provides the "Father's house" background.
"I am the gate; whoever enters through me will be saved" — the Gate saying that parallels the Way saying.
"Salvation is found in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given to mankind by which we must be saved" — Peter's application of the exclusivity of 14:6.

notes.note: "Do not let your hearts be troubled. You believe in God; believe also in me" — the opening of the Farewell Discourse is a pastoral comfort. The disciples are about to lose Jesus to what will look like defeat and abandonment. His first word is prohibition of anxiety; his second is expansion of faith. "Believe in God" — you already do this; "believe also in me" — extend that same trust to me, because I am going to the Father on your behalf.

"I am the way and the truth and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me" — this is the most exclusivist statement in John and one of the most contested in all of Christian theology. The claim is not that other religions have no truth or goodness but that personal access to the Father — the relational dimension of salvation — comes through Jesus alone. The claim is not about cultural superiority but about ontological necessity: since Jesus is the Father's unique Son and the only one who has seen the Father (1:18), access to the Father requires him as intermediary.

"Anyone who has seen me has seen the Father" — the most direct statement of the mutual indwelling. To know Jesus is to know the Father; to see Jesus is to see the Father. This does not mean Jesus and the Father are the same person (the discourse consistently distinguishes them) but that the Father is perfectly and completely revealed in the Son.

"Greater works than these... I will do whatever you ask in my name" — the greater works and the promise of prayer are connected: the disciples' mission will be enabled by Jesus's post-resurrection intercession. "In my name" is not a magic formula but a petition made in accordance with Jesus's character, purposes, and authority — the prayer of those who belong to him and seek what he seeks.

Calvin insists on the three-part structure: the way (the path of faith by which we come to God), the truth (the revelation that gives us true knowledge of God), and the life (the new life received through relationship with him). The three are not three separate gifts but three aspects of the single gift of knowing Christ. He is the way insofar as he leads us to the Father; he is the truth insofar as he reveals the Father; he is the life insofar as he communicates the Father's life to us.

Calvin resists the interpretation that "greater works" means more spectacular miracles. The greater works are the global mission: more people, more nations, more transformations. The apostolic mission exceeded Jesus's earthly ministry not in the quality of individual signs but in the breadth of the harvest. The Spirit poured out at Pentecost enables what the three-year Palestinian ministry could not in terms of geographic scope.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: "Many rooms" — NIV's translation of monai reflects the more accessible modern idiom; KJV's "mansions" has become misleading through association with opulent earthly dwellings. The word means "dwelling-places" or "resting-places." The emphasis is not on grandeur but on abundance (many) and permanence (monē from menō — to remain/abide).

"No one comes to the Father except through me" — the exclusive claim is absolute in the Greek (oudeis... ei mē di' emou). The context is personal access to the Father in relationship; the claim is about soteriology (how people are reconciled to God), not about metaphysics (who possesses some degree of truth). John consistently presents the Son as the unique mediator between the hidden God and humanity (1:18).

catena.source: Thomas Aquinas (compiler), Catena Aurea — Patristic Commentary, Public Domain

notes.note: Augustine: "I am the way, and the truth, and the life. The way by which you go, the truth to which you go, the life in which you remain. Walk by the way, and you will arrive at the truth. You will find rest in the life. By what way do you walk? By faith. What is your goal? Understanding. Why do you not faint on the way? Because life is with you."

Chrysostom: "He who has seen me has seen the Father. Not 'has seen the Father himself' — for the Father is not visible in the way the Son is. But he who has seen the Son has seen the one who sent him, because the Son perfectly manifests the Father. The Father is fully present in the Son; to know the Son is to know all that can be known of the Father."

hist.context: [('"I Am the Way" in Context', 'Thomas\'s literal question ("we don\'t know where you are going, so how can we know the way?") receives one of the most compact and radical answers in the Gospel. The way to the Father is not a route, a method, or a set of practices — it is a person. This is John\'s most direct exclusivism: Jesus is not the best way or the Jewish way or the uniquely authorised way — he is the only way ("no one comes to the Father except through me").'), ('"Greater Works"', 'The promise that believers will do "greater works" (v.12) has been the subject of debate. The greater works are most naturally the worldwide spread of the gospel: the disciples\' mission will encompass more people, more places, and more nations than Jesus\'s own three-year ministry in Palestine. The works are greater in scope (universal) rather than in kind (greater miracles).')]

sections.header: Verses 15–31 — The Promise of the Holy Spirit; Peace I Leave with You

heb.paragraph: The word (v.16, 26; 15:26; 16:7) means "one called alongside to help" — a legal advocate, a helper, a counsellor. Used of the Holy Spirit four times in the Farewell Discourse and once of Jesus himself (1 John 2:1). The Spirit is "another Advocate" (allon paraklēton, v.16) — another of the same kind as Jesus, who is the first Paraclete. The Spirit continues the work Jesus began, in the disciples rather than alongside them.

Jesus's gift of peace (v.27) is explicitly distinguished from the world's peace: "I do not give to you as the world gives." The world's peace is the absence of conflict; Jesus's peace is positive wellbeing rooted in relationship with God — the Hebrew shalom. This peace persists through tribulation (16:33: "I have overcome the world") rather than depending on its absence.

refs.note: "Unless I go away, the Advocate will not come to you; but if I go, I will send him to you" — the Spirit's coming is dependent on Jesus's departure.
Pentecost — the fulfilment of the Paraclete promises of the Farewell Discourse.
"The Spirit himself intercedes for us" — Paul's application of the Advocate theme.

notes.note: "Another Advocate... the Spirit of truth" — allon (another of the same kind) rather than heteron (another of a different kind). The Spirit continues Jesus's presence in the same mode: truth-speaking, comforting, advocating. "Lives with you and will be in you" — the transition from "with" (external presence, Jesus alongside) to "in" (internal presence, Spirit within) is the transition the entire Farewell Discourse prepares the disciples for.

"The Advocate, the Holy Spirit... will teach you all things and will remind you of everything I have said to you" — the two functions of the Spirit in relation to Scripture: teaching (new revelation) and reminding (accurate recall of Jesus's words). This verse is the foundation of the doctrine of inspiration: the NT witness to Jesus's words is Spirit-guaranteed because the Spirit was promised to remind the apostles of everything Jesus said.

"Peace I leave with you; my peace I give you" — the bequest language ("I leave") and the gift language ("I give") frame the peace as an inheritance and a present possession. The peace is "my peace" — the specific shalom that Jesus possessed in his own life of perfect trust in the Father. He gives not peace in general but the particular peace that characterised his own relationship with God.

"The Father is greater than I" — in the context of the whole Gospel, this statement is not an ontological demotion of the Son but a statement about the Son's present role. Jesus is going to the Father in the movement of incarnate obedience. The Father's greatness is the greatness of the one who sends over the one who is sent — a greatness of position in the economy, not of nature in the ontology.

Calvin uses the "remind" function of the Spirit (v.26) to address the accuracy of the Gospel traditions: the disciples could not have remembered Jesus's words perfectly by human memory alone; the Spirit's reminding function is what guarantees the accuracy of the Gospel accounts. This is not mechanical dictation but Spirit-enabled recollection — the apostles remembered under the Spirit's guidance what they had received from Jesus.

Calvin argues that "the Father is greater than I" refers to the Son's voluntary subordination in the economy of redemption, not to any essential inferiority. In the same way that the Son is described as "less" in the Incarnation in Phil 2:6-8 (though equal in essence), the Father's greatness in v.28 is the greatness of the sender over the sent, the undepleted over the self-emptied — a greatness of role, not of nature.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: "Another advocate" — the word allos (another of the same kind) rather than heteros (another of a different kind) is significant: the Spirit continues in exactly the same capacity Jesus fulfilled for the disciples. Jesus was their advocate/helper/counsellor; the Spirit will be the same, but internally rather than externally, permanently rather than temporally.

"The Father is greater than I" — this verse has been central to subordinationist Christologies (Arianism, contemporary Jehovah's Witnesses). The mainstream orthodox response is that the statement is economic (about the Son's incarnate role) not ontological (about the Son's eternal nature). The context supports this: Jesus is talking about going to the Father, which belongs to the narrative of the incarnate mission, not to the eternal divine relations.

catena.source: Thomas Aquinas (compiler), Catena Aurea — Patristic Commentary, Public Domain

notes.note: Augustine: "Another Advocate. Christ is our Advocate when he intercedes for us at the right hand of the Father. The Holy Spirit is our Advocate when he intercedes within us with sighs too deep for words. The Father hears the Son outside us; the Son hears the Spirit inside us; and the Father and Son together come to dwell where the Spirit prays."

Chrysostom: "My peace I give you — not the peace of the world, which is the mere absence of war. The world's peace is often the peace of the graveyard. My peace is the peace of the soul at rest in God, the peace that passes understanding, the peace that persists through tribulation, the peace that the world cannot give and cannot take away."

hist.context: [('The Paraclete Promises', "The five Paraclete sayings in the Farewell Discourse (14:16-17, 26; 15:26-27; 16:7-11, 13-15) are John's theology of the Holy Spirit. Together they describe the Spirit as: another Advocate (same kind as Jesus), the Spirit of truth, the one who teaches all things and reminds, the one who bears witness to Jesus, the one who convicts the world of sin/righteousness/judgment, and the one who guides into all truth. The Spirit's ministry is essentially to continue and apply the ministry of Jesus."), ('"The Father Is Greater Than I"', 'John 14:28 — "the Father is greater than I" — has been one of the most debated verses in Christological history. The subordinationist reading (Arius) took it as evidence of the Son\'s ontological inferiority. The orthodox reading (Athanasius, Calvin) takes it as a statement about the Son\'s present economic role: in the Incarnation and mission, the Son is functionally subordinate to the Father while being equal in essence. The context supports this: Jesus is going to the Father (economic return), which should cause them to rejoice (he is going to one who is in every sense his superior in the current arrangement).')]

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationmonai (14:2)NIV: "rooms"; KJV: "mansions"; ESV: "rooms"; NRSV: "dwelling places". KJV's "mansions" has been misleading; NIV/ESV's "rooms" better captures the sense of adequate personal space in a family home.paraklētos (14:16)NIV: "advocate"; KJV: "Comforter"; ESV: "Helper"; NRSV: "Advocate". Each captures one dimension: Advocate (legal representation), Comforter (pastoral care), Helper (practical assistance). No single English word captures all dimensions.allon (14:16)All versions render "another" without distinguishing allos from heteros. The theological point (another of the same kind as Jesus) requires commentary, not translation distinction.

src.title: Isaiah 9:6
src.quote: "And he will be called Wonderful Counsellor, Mighty God, Everlasting Father, Prince of Peace"
src.note: The Prince of Peace title that underlies Jesus's gift of peace in v.27 — the messianic peace-bearer promised in Isaiah.

src.title: Numbers 6:24-26
src.quote: "The Lord bless you and keep you... the Lord turn his face toward you and give you peace"
src.note: The Aaronic blessing whose peace Jesus claims to give in a new and personal form — shalom now given by the Son himself.

rec.who: "I Am the Way and the Truth and the Life" in Interfaith Dialogue
rec.text: John 14:6 is one of the most contested verses in contemporary interfaith relations. Its exclusive claim ("no one comes to the Father except through me") is seen by pluralist theologians as incompatible with respect for other religious traditions. Evangelical and Catholic scholars defend the exclusivity as intrinsic to the Christian gospel, while arguing that it does not preclude respectful dialogue or recognition of partial truth in other traditions. The verse was central to the Vatican II discussions about the relationship between Christianity and world religions and continues to shape the boundaries of Christian ecumenism.

rec.who: "In My Father's House Are Many Rooms" in Christian Hope
rec.text: John 14:2 has been one of the primary NT texts for Christian hope of heaven and reunion with the dead. Its image of a spacious dwelling prepared by Christ has shaped Christian funeral liturgy, hymnody, and devotion across two millennia. The KJV's "mansions" became so embedded in Christian imagination that it has outlived its accuracy — the word "rooms" (monai) is more accurate but less resonant. The assurance it offers — that Jesus goes ahead to prepare a place — has been the foundation of Christian comfort in the face of death.

rows.label: Comfort Bracket — vv.1, 27
rows.text: "Do not let your hearts be troubled" (v.1) and "do not let your hearts be troubled and do not be afraid" (v.27) bracket the chapter. The discourse begins and ends with the same command. The content between — the way to the Father, the Spirit's coming, the mutual indwelling — is the theological basis for the pastoral comfort.

rows.label: I Am the Way/Truth/Life as Triplet — v.6
rows.text: The triple I am predicate is structurally unique among the seven I am sayings. The three nouns are not synonyms but distinct dimensions: Way (relational access), Truth (revelatory content), Life (ontological gift). Together they encompass the entire scope of salvation.

rows.label: Promise / Problem / Answer Structure
rows.text: John 14 moves in a three-beat pattern: Jesus makes a promise (v1-3: I go to prepare), a disciple voices incomprehension (v5: Thomas; v8: Philip; v22: Judas), Jesus gives an expanded answer. This literary device forces a progressively deeper disclosure of who Jesus is.

hebtext.word: egō eimi hē hodos kai hē alētheia kai hē zōē
hebtext.tlit: e-GO ei-MI hē ho-DOS kai hē a-LE-thei-a kai hē ZO-ē
hebtext.gloss: I am the way and the truth and the life

thread.text: "I will dwell in the house of the Lord forever" — the Psalm that provides the "Father's house" background.
"I am the gate; whoever enters through me will be saved" — the Gate saying that parallels the Way saying.
"Salvation is found in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given to mankind by which we must be saved" — Peter's application of the exclusivity of 14:6.

"The Spirit himself intercedes for us" — Paul's application of the Advocate theme.

tx.title: "You may ask me for anything in my name"
tx.content: Some MSS omit "me" (reading "you may ask anything in my name"); others include it. NA28 includes "me." If "me" is original, it makes the prayer addressed to Jesus specifically, not just in his name to the Father — a direct prayer to the Son.
tx.note: The inclusion of "me" is well-attested. If original, it establishes prayer directed to Jesus as well as through him — significant for later trinitarian liturgy.

debate.title: "The Father is greater than I" — economic or ontological subordination?

positions.name: Economic subordination (incarnation only)
positions.argument: The Father's greatness refers to the Son's voluntary submission in the Incarnation. In the eternal relations, the Son is equal to the Father (1:1; 10:30); in the incarnate mission, the Son is functionally subordinate. The statement belongs to the economy, not the ontology.

positions.name: Eternal ontological subordination
positions.argument: The Father is ontologically greater — the Son is the first creation or a lesser divine being. The statement reflects the eternal order of being.

positions.name: Relational/personal subordination within equal essence
positions.argument: The eternal relations within the Trinity include a permanent relational order (Father-Son-Spirit) that involves authority/submission without ontological inequality. The Son eternally submits to the Father's authority while sharing the same divine nature.

themes.note: John 14 is the first of four Farewell Discourse chapters, and it establishes the theological foundation for what follows. Jesus comforts troubled disciples with the promise of a prepared place (vv.1-3), identifies himself as the exclusive path to the Father (v.6), reveals his unity with the Father (vv.9-11), promises the Advocate's coming (vv.16-17, 26), and bequeaths his peace (v.27). The whole discourse is structured around the disciples' anxiety about his departure and Jesus's systematic answer: you are not abandoned; another Advocate comes; the mutual indwelling continues; my peace remains with you.
